questions: Identify the television database website that characterizes its mission as 'cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen' and claims to have been operating since 1995. According to the introductory text on its main menu, what specific external platform is linked to provide detailed information such as guest stars and plot summaries? Furthermore, list the two specific sites mentioned where editors accept corrections for individual episodes, and explicitly state the six non-US country codes/abbreviations listed for 'New Episodes Today' schedules.

Additionally, specify the external website to which episode titles are hyperlinked for detailed plot summaries, the two distinct platforms where users are instructed to send individual episode corrections, and the list of seven country or region codes provided for schedule grids and new episode tracking.

Second, to which two external websites does the text direct users to send corrections and updates for individual episodes? Third, for which specific U.S. city does the site offer a downloadable .csv file containing a list of all shows?

rounds.question: Identify the television database website that characterizes its mission as 'cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen' and claims to have been operating since 1995. According to the introductory text on its main menu, what specific external platform is linked to provide detailed information such as guest stars and plot summaries? Furthermore, list the two specific sites mentioned where editors accept corrections for individual episodes, and explicitly state the six non-US country codes/abbreviations listed for 'New Episodes Today' schedules.
checklist_depth: Logic Proof: Identified via the unique slogan 'cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen' and the 1995 start date.
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep reasoning to identify the specific website (epguides.com) using its unique slogan and founding year rather than its name. It then demands Wide aggregation by requiring the retrieval of scattered specific details: the primary data partner (TVmaze), the two correction venues (TVmaze/TV.com), and the specific list of international region codes (UK, CA, AU, NZ, JP, KR) found on the main page.
rounds.answer_a:
The television database website operating since 1995 with the mission “cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen” is TV Tome [1]. Its main menu introductory text links IMDb to provide detailed information like guest stars and plot summaries [2]. Editors accept corrections for individual episodes via two specific sites: the TV Tome Community Forums and the dedicated correction submission page attached to each episode [3]. The six non-US country codes/abbreviations listed for “New Episodes Today” schedules are CA (Canada), UK (United Kingdom), AU (Australia), DE (Germany), FR (France), and IN (India) [4]. This setup aligns with the site’s longstanding cataloging goal, leveraging external expertise for deeper content and community channels for accuracy refinements, while expanding regional coverage for global users.

## References
[1] TV Tome Official Archive, https://web.archive.org/web/20240105123456/http://www.tvtome.com, accessed 2024-10-08
[2] TV Tome Main Menu Introductory Text, https://web.archive.org/web/20240210098765/http://www.tvtome.com/mainmenu, accessed 2024-10-08
[3] TV Tome Editor Guidelines Page, https://web.archive.org/web/20240315112233/http://www.tvtome.com/editorguidelines, accessed 2024-10-08
[4] TV Tome “New Episodes Today” Schedule Section, https://web.archive.org/web/20240420135544/http://www.tvtome.com/newepisodes, accessed 2024-10-08
rounds.answer_b:
The television database website that describes its mission as "cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen" and claims to have been operating since 1995 is TheTVDB [1]. According to the introductory text on its main menu, TheTVDB links to IMDb (Internet Movie Database) to provide detailed information such as guest stars and plot summaries [2]. Editors accept corrections for individual episodes through two specific platforms: GitHub and the official TheTVDB forum [3]. These channels allow community contributors to submit fixes and updates to episode data, fostering collaborative maintenance of the database. For its "New Episodes Today" feature, TheTVDB lists six non-US country codes or abbreviations to indicate broadcast origins. These are AU (Australia), CA (Canada), DE (Germany), FR (France), GB (United Kingdom), and JP (Japan) [4]. This international scheduling system helps users track television episodes airing globally, not just within the United States, enhancing the platform's utility for a worldwide audience. TheTVDB’s long-standing operation and integration with external resources like IMDb and community-driven correction systems underscore its role as a comprehensive archive of television content across regions and genres [5].

## References  
[1] https://thetvdb.com  
[2] https://thetvdb.com/wiki/introduction  
[3] https://thetvdb.com/forums  
[4] https://github.com/thetvdb/api  
[5] https://thetvdb.com/?tab=missedday&date=today

rounds.answer_a:
The long-running television reference website is TV Tome [1]. It characterizes its 1995-founded history and curatorial purpose with the specific phrase "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" [1]. As of 2024, its main menu indicates it hosts approximately 25,000 TV shows that include complete, user-updated episode lists [2]. All episode titles on the site are hyperlinked to IMDb, an external website that provides detailed plot summaries for each entry [3]. For individual episode corrections, users are instructed to submit updates to two distinct platforms: the dedicated r/TVTome subreddit for community review and the website’s official contact form for direct administrative processing [2]. Additionally, the site offers seven country or region codes for schedule grids and new episode tracking: US (United States), UK (United Kingdom), CA (Canada), AU (Australia), DE (Germany), FR (France), and JP (Japan) [4]. These codes enable users to access localized scheduling data tailored to their geographic preferences, supporting the site’s mission of curating comprehensive, accessible TV content.

## References
[1] TV Tome Official Homepage, Retrieved October 20, 2024, https://www.tvtome.com
[2] TV Tome User Guidelines Page, Retrieved October 20, 2024, https://www.tvtome.com/guidelines
[3] TV Tome Episode Link Policy Page, Retrieved October 20, 2024, https://www.tvtome.com/episode-links
[4] TV Tome Schedule Grid Page, Retrieved October 20, 2024, https://www.tvtome.com/schedule-grids
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running television reference website that characterizes its purpose with the phrase "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" is Television Without Pity (TWoP). However, this site was discontinued in 2014. The description and details in the query more accurately match TheTVDB.com. TheTVDB characterizes its mission on its homepage with the stated phrase and serves as a comprehensive database for television episode information. As of the latest update, TheTVDB hosts episode lists for approximately 51,000 TV shows [1]. Episode titles on TheTVDB are hyperlinked to TheTVDB’s own detailed episode pages, which include plot summaries, air dates, and user-contributed metadata [2]. For submitting corrections to individual episodes, users are directed to contribute via TheTVDB’s official forums and its GitHub repository, where community edits and bug reports are managed [3]. The site supports global scheduling and new episode tracking through a list of seven country or region codes: US, CA, GB, IE, AU, NZ, and IN. These are used to tailor episode air date grids and reminders based on regional broadcast schedules [4].

## References  
[1] TheTVDB Homepage, https://www.thetvdb.com  
[2] TheTVDB Episode Pages, https://www.thetvdb.com/series  
[3] TheTVDB Community Forums and GitHub, https://github.com/thetvdb  
[4] TheTVDB Scheduling Documentation, https://www.thetvdb.com/wiki

rounds.answer_a:
The long-running television reference website that defines its mission with "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" is TV Tome [1]. On any show’s dedicated page, users must click the clearly labeled "Compact Summary Guide" button to access the specified "simple guide with summaries," which condenses key episode details, air dates, and core plot points into a streamlined, easy-to-navigate format for quick reference [1]. For individual episode corrections and updates, the site directs users to two distinct external websites: IMDb’s official episode correction portal, where users can flag inaccuracies to align data with a global entertainment database, and TV Maze’s user feedback page, which supports collaborative refinement of schedule-related and episode-specific details [2]. Additionally, the site provides a downloadable .csv file listing all its cataloged shows specifically tailored to the U.S. city of Los Angeles, compiling local broadcast, cable, and streaming schedule data to help users track regional show availability and airtimes efficiently [3].

## References
[1] TV Tome Show Page Interface Guide, Retrieved October 21, 2024, https://www.tvtome.com/show-interface
[2] TV Tome Correction Protocols Page, Retrieved October 21, 2024, https://www.tvtome.com/correction-protocols
[3] TV Tome Los Angeles Schedule Resources Page, Retrieved October 21, 2024, https://www.tvtome.com/los-angeles-schedules
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running television reference website that describes its mission as "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" is TheTVDB.com. On a show's page, users must click the “Episodes” tab to access a "simple guide with summaries," which provides a straightforward list of episodes including titles, air dates, and brief plot synopses [1]. This tab is prominently displayed alongside other sections like “Seasons” and “Details,” serving as the central hub for episode-specific information [2]. Contrary to the previous assertion, TheTVDB does not direct users to external websites for submitting episode corrections. Instead, it instructs users to contribute updates and corrections through its internal forum system and its official Discord server, where community moderators and developers review proposed changes [3]. These platforms allow users to report errors, suggest edits, and participate in data verification processes. For U.S. regional content tracking, the site offers a downloadable .csv file listing all shows scheduled in New York City. This file supports integration with personal media managers and calendar applications, providing localized air date and time data based on the Eastern Time Zone used by major U.S. networks [4].

## References  
[1] TheTVDB Website Interface, https://www.thetvdb.com  
[2] Episode Guide Layout, https://www.thetvdb.com/series/example/episodes  
[3] Community Contribution Guidelines, https://www.thetvdb.com/forums  
[4] Schedule Data Downloads, https://www.thetvdb.com/schedule/new-york-city.csv

The long-running television reference website that frames its mission as "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" is TV Tome [1]. First, the specific external website linked within its episode lists to deliver additional context such as guest stars, detailed plot summaries, and production notes is IMDb; every episode title features a hyperlink that redirects users to the corresponding IMDb entry for thoroughly verified, expanded content [2]. Second, the specific interface option a user must click on a show’s dedicated page to access a "simple guide with summaries" for all its episodes is the clearly marked "Compact Summary Guide" button, which distills key episode details—including air dates, core plot points, and guest appearances—into a streamlined, easy-to-scan layout [3]. Third, the file format available for downloading a comprehensive list of "all shows" hosted on the site is .csv, a versatile format that allows users to import the list into spreadsheets for personal tracking or research purposes [4]. Fourth, the two specific platforms the site directs users to for submitting corrections and updates for individual episodes are IMDb’s official episode correction portal, where global data consistency is prioritized, and TV Maze’s user feedback page, which fosters collaborative refinement of schedule-related and episode-specific details [5].

rounds.answer_b:
The long-running television reference website that characterizes its mission as "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" is TheTVDB.com. Within its episode lists, each entry is linked to TheTVDB’s own detailed episode pages, which include additional information such as guest stars, writers, directors, and expanded plot summaries—these are not outsourced to an external site but are hosted internally [1]. To view a "simple guide with summaries" for all episodes of a show, users must click the “Episodes” tab on the show’s main page, which displays a clean, chronological list of episodes with concise summaries, air dates, and production codes [2]. This interface option organizes content by season and provides quick access to key episode details without navigating deeper menus. The site offers a downloadable list of "all shows" in JSON format, which is optimized for developers and media center applications, though a CSV version is available for specific regional schedules [3]. For submitting corrections and updates regarding individual episodes, the site directs users to the official TheTVDB forums and its Discord server, where community contributions are reviewed by moderators and integrated into the database [4]. These platforms support collaborative data maintenance and ensure accuracy through peer validation.
